state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Roast My Project (Telegram Bot) — Brutally Honest, Constructive Startup Critic

Summary:
A Telegram bot that delivers sharp, witty, but non-toxic product critiques for startup ideas, landing pages, and early-stage projects. Users submit a short description and optionally a link; the bot returns a structured roast under 500 words: first impression, problem clarity, differentiation, business model, target market focus, UX/positioning critique (if link), the single biggest red flag, 3–5 specific actionable improvements, a Roast Score (1–10), and a mic-drop closing line.

The system is designed as a single Node.js (ESM) service using grammY, with MongoDB for persistence and lightweight in-memory maps for transient conversation state. It supports safe, policy-aligned “slightly savage” tone via guardrails, opt-in intensity, and user controls. The design prioritizes production readiness: rate limiting, abuse prevention, consistent formatting, reliable retries, telemetry, and clear operational boundaries.

Product goals:
Primary user value
1) Turn vague startup ideas into immediate, actionable clarity by delivering a structured critique that feels entertaining and honest.
2) Provide consistent coverage of key evaluation axes: first impression, problem clarity, differentiation, business model realism, market focus, and (when a link is included) messaging/UX positioning.
3) Keep responses short and high-signal: under 500 words, zero fluff, specific fixes, and a clear “biggest red flag.”

Tone and safety goals
1) The bot is “slightly savage” but never insulting: no harassment, slurs, or personal attacks; critique is aimed at the project, not the person.
2) The bot defaults to constructive bluntness, and optionally allows users to dial intensity (e.g., “mild / medium / savage”) with constraints that still prevent toxicity.
3) The bot avoids making up facts about the user’s business; it labels assumptions clearly.

Product scope boundaries (for production)
1) The bot critiques what it can observe in the user’s submission and (if link) the page content it can fetch; it does not provide legal/medical/financial advice beyond general business commentary.
2) It does not store sensitive data unnecessarily; it stores submissions and responses only for history, user convenience, and abuse analysis, with retention controls.
3) It supports fast onboarding: one command to start, one flow to submit, one clear output format.

High-level architecture:
Service layout (single Node.js process)
1) Telegram interface layer (grammY): command handlers, conversational scenes, callback query handlers, and message formatting enforcement (word count, section ordering).
2) Orchestration layer: takes a normalized submission object and produces a roast result via: content retrieval (optional), prompt assembly, safety/tone checks, generation, post-processing, and persistence.
3) Content retrieval layer (for links): fetches HTML, extracts text and key page signals (headline, subheadline, CTA text, social proof indicators). Includes timeouts, size limits, and robots-respecting behavior as appropriate.
4) Persistence layer (MongoDB): users, submissions, roasts, settings, and moderation/rate-limit counters.

Conversation handling
1) Use a guided flow to reduce garbage inputs: ask for project description, target user, current stage, monetization idea, and optional link.
2) Accept “single message submission” as a shortcut: user pastes everything; the bot parses it and asks only missing fields.
3) Maintain ephemeral state for in-progress sessions using in-memory maps keyed by user id, with TTL cleanup; persist completed submissions in MongoDB.

Generation pipeline (LLM-agnostic design)
1) Input normalization: trim, de-duplicate, detect link(s), language, and length; reject extremely short or empty submissions.
2) Page analysis (if link): fetch and extract: headline, above-the-fold messaging, CTA(s), pricing visibility, trust signals, and obvious friction points. If fetch fails, proceed without it and state limitation.
3) Prompt assembly: strict response schema and constraints: max 500 words, required sections, “biggest red flag,” 3–5 actionable changes, Roast Score 1–10, mic-drop line, no personal insults.
4) Output enforcement: automatic checks for word count, missing sections, toxicity markers, and hallucinated claims; if violations occur, run a constrained rewrite pass.

Operational controls
1) Rate limiting per user and per chat: prevent spam and cost explosions; allow higher limits for whitelisted users.
2) Abuse controls: blocklist users, detect repeated prompt injection attempts, and force “mild” mode for flagged users.
3) Observability: structured logs, request ids, timing metrics for fetch/generation, and error categorization to support on-call debugging.

Reliability
1) All external calls (content fetch, generation) have timeouts, retries with jitter, and clear fallbacks.
2) Idempotency: submissions get unique ids; if Telegram retries updates or users double-send, the bot avoids duplicating roasts.
3) Backpressure: if generation is slow, the bot sends a “working on it” message and edits/updates when ready.

Data model:
MongoDB collections (suggested)
1) users
1) telegramUserId, username, createdAt, lastSeenAt
2) settings: roastIntensity (mild/medium/savage), language, linkFetchingEnabled, privacy (storeHistory true/false)
3) counters: dailyRoastsCount, lastRoastAt
4) moderation: status (active/limited/banned), notes

2) submissions
1) submissionId, userId, chatId, createdAt
2) rawText (original user message) and parsedFields: projectName (optional), description, targetUser, problem, differentiationClaim, businessModel, stage, link
3) linkMetadata: fetchStatus, fetchedAt, contentHash, extractedSignals (headline, CTA text, trust signals summary), truncatedPageText (optional)

3) roasts
1) roastId, submissionId, userId, createdAt
2) roastText (final), roastScore (1–10), biggestRedFlag (string)
3) qualityFlags: overWordLimitFixed, missingSectionFixed, toxicityRewriteApplied, linkUnavailable
4) modelMetadata: provider name (string), latencyMs, tokenEstimates (optional)

4) events (optional but useful)
1) eventId, userId, type (commandused, roastgenerated, error, ratelimited), createdAt
2) payload (small JSON for debugging; avoid storing sensitive content)

In-memory state (ephemeral)
1) activeSessions: userId -> session object containing current step, partial fields, startedAt
2) cooldowns: userId -> timestamps to enforce short-term throttles

Retention and privacy
1) Support a user setting to disable storing submission content; if off, store minimal metadata only (timestamps, score) for rate limiting.
2) Add a cleanup job within the process (timer-based) to purge old submissions/roasts past retention (e.g., 30–90 days) when enabled.

Key flows:
1) Onboarding and tone calibration
1) /start explains what the bot does, the “slightly savage but constructive” promise, and the 500-word constraint.
2) User chooses intensity (default medium). The bot clarifies that insults are not allowed; critiques are about the product.
3) Bot suggests the submission format and offers a “one-message template.”

2) Guided submission (recommended)
1) /roast starts a session.
2) Bot asks for: what it is (1–2 sentences), who it’s for, what pain it solves, why it’s different, how it makes money, and optional link.
3) Bot validates minimum completeness (e.g., at least description + target user). If missing, it asks a single clarifying question.
4) Bot confirms: “I’ll roast this with intensity X; link analysis Y; under 500 words.”

3) Single-message submission (fast path)
1) User sends a paragraph and a link.
2) Bot parses the message, extracts link, detects missing core fields.
3) If fields are missing, it asks at most one follow-up question to avoid user fatigue.

4) Link critique (when provided)
1) Bot fetches the page with strict limits: small maximum bytes, short timeout, and a safe user agent.
2) Bot extracts key positioning elements: headline clarity, subheadline support, CTA presence, trust signals, pricing visibility, and friction.
3) Bot integrates findings into the roast under a dedicated UX/positioning section.
4) If fetch fails, bot says so plainly and critiques based on the user-provided description only.

5) Roast generation and enforcement
1) Bot generates a structured roast with required components:
1) Strong first impression
2) Problem clarity
3) Differentiation and switching motivation
4) Business model realism
5) Target market focus
6) UX/positioning critique (if link)
7) Single biggest red flag
8) 3–5 specific actionable improvements
9) Roast Score (1–10)
10) Mic-drop line
2) Post-processing checks:
1) Word count <= 500
2) Contains all required sections
3) No personal attacks; no hate/harassment
4) Action items are concrete (e.g., change headline, add pricing, narrow ICP)
3) If a check fails, the bot performs a constrained rewrite pass and re-validates.

6) History, sharing, and iteration
1) /history lists recent roasts with short titles and scores.
2) User can tap “Roast it again (harder/softer)” which reuses the same submission with a different intensity setting.
3) User can reply with “What if we target X instead?” and the bot offers a delta-roast focused on the changed assumption.

7) Moderation and abuse handling
1) If user attempts to force toxicity or personal attacks, the bot refuses and proceeds with a safer critique.
2) If the user spams, bot rate-limits and explains when they can try again.
3) Admin-only controls allow limiting/banning abusive users and reviewing usage metrics.

Tech stack:
Runtime and bot framework
1) Node.js (ESM) single service process.
2) grammY for Telegram update handling, middleware, and conversational flows.
3) Configuration via environment variables, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.

Persistence
1) MongoDB (already integrated) for durable storage of users, submissions, roasts, and moderation state.
2) In-memory maps for short-lived session state and cooldowns (with TTL cleanup) to keep flows responsive.

External integrations (kept generic)
1) A text generation provider to produce the roast; the bot is designed so the provider can be swapped without changing business logic.
2) Optional: object storage or simple filesystem storage only if you later support user-uploaded screenshots or cached page snapshots; otherwise not required.

HTTP and content retrieval
1) A simple HTTP client for fetching public web pages with strict size/time limits.
2) An HTML-to-text extraction approach to derive headline/CTA/trust signals for the UX critique.

Deployment and operations
1) Run as a Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Use either long polling or webhook mode depending on your environment; the architecture supports both.
3) Centralized logs and basic metrics exported from the process (latency, error rates, rate-limits, generation time).

Non-functional requirements:
Security and privacy
1) Minimize stored data: store only what’s needed for history and abuse prevention; allow users to disable history.
2) Treat links as untrusted input: enforce allow/deny rules (e.g., block local network targets), timeouts, and response size caps.
3) Protect tokens and secrets via environment variables; avoid printing sensitive content in logs.

Safety and tone guarantees
1) The bot never targets immutable characteristics or the user personally; it critiques the product and messaging.
2) Built-in refusal patterns for requests to harass, doxx, or generate hateful content.
3) If user requests “more savage,” the bot increases bluntness and humor, not cruelty.

Performance and scalability
1) Limit concurrent roasts per process to avoid memory spikes; queue internally using in-process concurrency control.
2) Cache link fetch results briefly (content hash + TTL) to avoid repeated downloads during “roast again.”
3) Keep responses compact and consistent; always enforce the 500-word maximum.

Reliability and maintainability
1) Clear error handling paths: link fetch failure, generation timeout, Telegram API errors.
2) Idempotent handling of updates and retries.
3) Structured logging with correlation ids per submission.

Product quality
1) Output consistency enforced by schema-like validation (section presence, word count, actionable items).
2) A/B testable system prompts and tone settings stored in config so you can iterate without redeploying frequently.
3) Basic analytics: counts of roasts, average score distribution, most common red flags (derived tags) to improve prompts and UX.

Bot commands:
• /start — Explain what the bot does, set expectations, and guide the first roast. (usage: /start) [Onboarding]
• /roast — Start a guided flow to submit your project and receive a structured roast. (usage: /roast) [Core]
• /settings — Configure roast intensity, language, link fetching, and history privacy. (usage: /settings) [Configuration]
• /intensity — Quickly change roast intensity (mild, medium, savage). (usage: /intensity mild|medium|savage) [Configuration]
• /template — Get a one-message submission template you can paste and fill in. (usage: /template) [Core]
• /history — View your recent roasts and re-run one with different intensity. (usage: /history) [Utility]
• /redo — Re-roast your last submission (optionally with a new intensity). (usage: /redo [mild|medium|savage]) [Core]
• /deletemydata — Delete stored history and disable future storage (optional privacy mode). (usage: /deletemydata) [Privacy]
• /help — Show usage tips, examples of good submissions, and limits. (usage: /help) [Onboarding]
• /status — Show service status hints (rate limits, last roast time) for the user. (usage: /status) [Utility]

DOCS.md.content:
What this bot does
Roast My Project is a Telegram bot that gives short, investor-style analysis of startup and project pitches. It focuses on TAM logic, CAC/payback reality, defensibility/moat, and a plausible GTM wedge. Replies are intentionally compact for fast Telegram reading.

Public commands
1) /start
What it does: Explains what to submit to get the best VC-style analysis.
Usage: /start

2) /help
What it does: Shows examples and the exact output format.
Usage: /help

3) /reset
What it does: Clears your stored long-term memory (conversation turns) for this chat.
Usage: /reset

How to use
Send 2–8 sentences describing your project. If you want the sharpest output, include:
1) ICP / buyer (who pays)
2) Pricing (or ACV)
3) Acquisition channel (how customers discover/buy)
Optional: traction and a URL.

Output format (compact VC-style)
Every response follows this exact order:
A) Verdict: 1–2 sentences
B) Roast Score: 1–10 with one-sentence justification
C) TAM: numbers/ranges when possible; if unknown, a labeled assumption-based estimate
D) CAC and payback: channel constraints + one key target metric when possible
E) Moat: 1–3 short numbered lines (1) 2) 3))
F) GTM: single most plausible channel + wedge; one sentence
G) Biggest risk: one sentence
H) Next step: one concrete action
I) Up to 2 questions: only if truly needed to unblock TAM/CAC/moat

Question-asking rule
If your message already contains ICP + pricing + acquisition channel (or close equivalents), the bot will not ask questions.
If critical info is missing, it will ask at most 2 targeted questions at the end.

Environment variables
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN (required)
Used to connect to Telegram.

2) COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT (required)
CookMyBots AI Gateway base URL. Example: https://api.cookmybots.com/api/ai
Important: do not include /chat in this value.

3) COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY (required)
CookMyBots AI Gateway key used for Authorization: Bearer.

4) MONGODB_URI (optional)
If set, the bot stores conversation turns in MongoDB in the memory_messages collection. If missing, it falls back to in-memory storage and logs a warning.

5) AI_TIMEOUT_MS (optional)
AI call timeout in milliseconds. Default is 600000.

Run instructions
1) npm install
2) Set env vars (see .env.sample)
3) npm run dev

README.md.content:
Roast My Project is a Telegram bot that gives short, VC-style analysis of startup pitches. Users paste a short description (optionally a URL) and the bot replies with a compact investor-style evaluation and a 1–10 Roast Score.

Setup
1) Install dependencies: npm install
2) Create a .env file (or set env vars in your host) using .env.sample as a reference
3) Run locally: npm run dev
4) Run in production: npm start

Environment variables
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN (required) Telegram bot token
2) COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT (required) CookMyBots AI Gateway base URL (must NOT include /chat)
3) COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY (required) CookMyBots AI Gateway key
4) MONGODB_URI (optional) Enables long-term memory storage in MongoDB. If missing, the bot uses an in-memory fallback.
5) AI_TIMEOUT_MS (optional) AI request timeout in ms. Default 600000.

Commands
1) /start: what to send and how this bot behaves
2) /help: examples and output format
3) /reset: clear your stored memory for this chat

Output format
Every response follows this order:
A) Verdict
B) Roast Score (1–10)
C) TAM
D) CAC and payback
E) Moat (1–3 numbered lines)
F) GTM (single channel + wedge)
G) Biggest risk
H) Next step
I) Up to 2 questions (only if needed)

Tips for best results
Include: ICP (who buys), pricing/ACV, acquisition channel, and any traction. If those are present, the bot will not ask follow-up questions.

Notes on reliability
The bot uses long polling via @grammyjs/runner with concurrency=1, clears any webhook at boot, and retries on Telegram 409 conflicts. It includes per-user in-flight locks and a small global cap to prevent memory build-up.

export default function register(bot) {
  bot.command("help", async (ctx) => {
    await ctx.reply(
      [
        "Send a short pitch and I’ll reply in a compact investor format.",
        "Best inputs include: ICP, pricing/ACV, and acquisition channel (plus any traction).",
        "",
        "Examples:",
        "1) “We automate insurance verification for dental clinics. Buyer: office manager. $299/mo/location. Channel: partnerships with PMS consultants. 12 paying clinics.”",
        "2) “AI triage for support inboxes. ICP: 50–500 seat SaaS support teams. $40/agent/mo. Channel: Zendesk marketplace + outbound to support leaders.”",
        "",
        "Output sections (always in this order):",
        "A) Verdict",
        "B) Roast Score (1–10)",
        "C) TAM",
        "D) CAC and payback",
        "E) Moat",
        "F) GTM",
        "G) Biggest risk",
        "H) Next step",
        "I) Up to 2 questions (only if needed)",
        "",
        "Use /reset to clear your stored memory for this chat.",
      ].join("\n")
    );
  });
}

aiMessages.content: You are “Roast My Project,” a brutally honest but constructive startup critic whose job is to evaluate a user’s project, product, landing page, or business idea with sharp, witty, intelligent feedback that is slightly savage but never toxic or insulting; analyze the submission by giving a strong first impression, assessing problem clarity (is the problem real, painful, and clearly defined?), differentiation (what makes it different and why users would switch?), business model (how it realistically makes money), target market focus (too broad or niche enough), and if a link is provided, critique UX and positioning including headline strength, messaging clarity, CTA effectiveness, and trust signals; identify the single biggest red flag, provide 3–5 specific and actionable improvements (no generic advice like “improve marketing”), assign a Roast Score from 1–10 where 1 means “This will die quietly” and 10 means “Annoyingly good idea,” avoid fluff and sugarcoating, keep it under 500 words, and end with a short mic-drop closing line.

Created Roast Critic as a full Telegram bot with command modules (/start, /help, /reset) registered via a loader, and an AI roast agent in src/features/agent.js registered after commands. Added CookMyBots AI Gateway client (src/lib/ai.js) with start/success/failure logs and timeout, MongoDB-backed long-term memory (memory_messages) with in-memory fallback, Bot Profile injection into every AI request, and production-safe polling via @grammyjs/runner with deleteWebhook, 409 conflict backoff, process-level crash guards, and in-flight locks/backpressure.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Roast My Project (Telegram Bot) — Brutally Honest, Constructive Startup Critic

Summary:
A Telegram bot that delivers sharp, witty, but non-toxic product critiques for startup ideas, landing pages, and early-stage projects. Users submit a short description and optionally a link; the bot returns a structured roast under 500 words: first impression, problem clarity, differentiation, business model, target market focus, UX/positioning critique (if link), the single biggest red flag, 3–5 specific actionable improvements, a Roast Score (1–10), and a mic-drop closing line.

The system is designed as a single Node.js (ESM) service using grammY, with MongoDB for persistence and lightweight in-memory maps for transient conversation state. It supports safe, policy-aligned “slightly savage” tone via guardrails, opt-in intensity, and user controls. The design prioritizes production readiness: rate limiting, abuse prevention, consistent formatting, reliable retries, telemetry, and clear operational boundaries.

Product goals:
Primary user value
1) Turn vague startup ideas into immediate, actionable clarity by delivering a structured critique that feels entertaining and honest.
2) Provide consistent coverage of key evaluation axes: first impression, problem clarity, differentiation, business model realism, market focus, and (when a link is included) messaging/UX positioning.
3) Keep responses short and high-signal: under 500 words, zero fluff, specific fixes, and a clear “biggest red flag.”

Tone and safety goals
1) The bot is “slightly savage” but never insulting: no harassment, slurs, or personal attacks; critique is aimed at the project, not the person.
2) The bot defaults to constructive bluntness, and optionally allows users to dial intensity (e.g., “mild / medium / savage”) with constraints that still prevent toxicity.
3) The bot avoids making up facts about the user’s business; it labels assumptions clearly.

Product scope boundaries (for production)
1) The bot critiques what it can observe in the user’s submission and (if link) the page content it can fetch; it does not provide legal/medical/financial advice beyond general business commentary.
2) It does not store sensitive data unnecessarily; it stores submissions and responses only for history, user convenience, and abuse analysis, with retention controls.
3) It supports fast onboarding: one command to start, one flow to submit, one clear output format.

High-level architecture:
Service layout (single Node.js process)
1) Telegram interface layer (grammY): command handlers, conversational scenes, callback query handlers, and message formatting enforcement (word count, section ordering).
2) Orchestration layer: takes a normalized submission object and produces a roast result via: content retrieval (optional), prompt assembly, safety/tone checks, generation, post-processing, and persistence.
3) Content retrieval layer (for links): fetches HTML, extracts text and key page signals (headline, subheadline, CTA text, social proof indicators). Includes timeouts, size limits, and robots-respecting behavior as appropriate.
4) Persistence layer (MongoDB): users, submissions, roasts, settings, and moderation/rate-limit counters.

Conversation handling
1) Use a guided flow to reduce garbage inputs: ask for project description, target user, current stage, monetization idea, and optional link.
2) Accept “single message submission” as a shortcut: user pastes everything; the bot parses it and asks only missing fields.
3) Maintain ephemeral state for in-progress sessions using in-memory maps keyed by user id, with TTL cleanup; persist completed submissions in MongoDB.

Generation pipeline (LLM-agnostic design)
1) Input normalization: trim, de-duplicate, detect link(s), language, and length; reject extremely short or empty submissions.
2) Page analysis (if link): fetch and extract: headline, above-the-fold messaging, CTA(s), pricing visibility, trust signals, and obvious friction points. If fetch fails, proceed without it and state limitation.
3) Prompt assembly: strict response schema and constraints: max 500 words, required sections, “biggest red flag,” 3–5 actionable changes, Roast Score 1–10, mic-drop line, no personal insults.
4) Output enforcement: automatic checks for word count, missing sections, toxicity markers, and hallucinated claims; if violations occur, run a constrained rewrite pass.

Operational controls
1) Rate limiting per user and per chat: prevent spam and cost explosions; allow higher limits for whitelisted users.
2) Abuse controls: blocklist users, detect repeated prompt injection attempts, and force “mild” mode for flagged users.
3) Observability: structured logs, request ids, timing metrics for fetch/generation, and error categorization to support on-call debugging.

Reliability
1) All external calls (content fetch, generation) have timeouts, retries with jitter, and clear fallbacks.
2) Idempotency: submissions get unique ids; if Telegram retries updates or users double-send, the bot avoids duplicating roasts.
3) Backpressure: if generation is slow, the bot sends a “working on it” message and edits/updates when ready.

Data model:
MongoDB collections (suggested)
1) users
1) telegramUserId, username, createdAt, lastSeenAt
2) settings: roastIntensity (mild/medium/savage), language, linkFetchingEnabled, privacy (storeHistory true/false)
3) counters: dailyRoastsCount, lastRoastAt
4) moderation: status (active/limited/banned), notes

2) submissions
1) submissionId, userId, chatId, createdAt
2) rawText (original user message) and parsedFields: projectName (optional), description, targetUser, problem, differentiationClaim, businessModel, stage, link
3) linkMetadata: fetchStatus, fetchedAt, contentHash, extractedSignals (headline, CTA text, trust signals summary), truncatedPageText (optional)

3) roasts
1) roastId, submissionId, userId, createdAt
2) roastText (final), roastScore (1–10), biggestRedFlag (string)
3) qualityFlags: overWordLimitFixed, missingSectionFixed, toxicityRewriteApplied, linkUnavailable
4) modelMetadata: provider name (string), latencyMs, tokenEstimates (optional)

4) events (optional but useful)
1) eventId, userId, type (commandused, roastgenerated, error, ratelimited), createdAt
2) payload (small JSON for debugging; avoid storing sensitive content)

In-memory state (ephemeral)
1) activeSessions: userId -> session object containing current step, partial fields, startedAt
2) cooldowns: userId -> timestamps to enforce short-term throttles

Retention and privacy
1) Support a user setting to disable storing submission content; if off, store minimal metadata only (timestamps, score) for rate limiting.
2) Add a cleanup job within the process (timer-based) to purge old submissions/roasts past retention (e.g., 30–90 days) when enabled.

Key flows:
1) Onboarding and tone calibration
1) /start explains what the bot does, the “slightly savage but constructive” promise, and the 500-word constraint.
2) User chooses intensity (default medium). The bot clarifies that insults are not allowed; critiques are about the product.
3) Bot suggests the submission format and offers a “one-message template.”

2) Guided submission (recommended)
1) /roast starts a session.
2) Bot asks for: what it is (1–2 sentences), who it’s for, what pain it solves, why it’s different, how it makes money, and optional link.
3) Bot validates minimum completeness (e.g., at least description + target user). If missing, it asks a single clarifying question.
4) Bot confirms: “I’ll roast this with intensity X; link analysis Y; under 500 words.”

3) Single-message submission (fast path)
1) User sends a paragraph and a link.
2) Bot parses the message, extracts link, detects missing core fields.
3) If fields are missing, it asks at most one follow-up question to avoid user fatigue.

4) Link critique (when provided)
1) Bot fetches the page with strict limits: small maximum bytes, short timeout, and a safe user agent.
2) Bot extracts key positioning elements: headline clarity, subheadline support, CTA presence, trust signals, pricing visibility, and friction.
3) Bot integrates findings into the roast under a dedicated UX/positioning section.
4) If fetch fails, bot says so plainly and critiques based on the user-provided description only.

5) Roast generation and enforcement
1) Bot generates a structured roast with required components:
1) Strong first impression
2) Problem clarity
3) Differentiation and switching motivation
4) Business model realism
5) Target market focus
6) UX/positioning critique (if link)
7) Single biggest red flag
8) 3–5 specific actionable improvements
9) Roast Score (1–10)
10) Mic-drop line
2) Post-processing checks:
1) Word count <= 500
2) Contains all required sections
3) No personal attacks; no hate/harassment
4) Action items are concrete (e.g., change headline, add pricing, narrow ICP)
3) If a check fails, the bot performs a constrained rewrite pass and re-validates.

6) History, sharing, and iteration
1) /history lists recent roasts with short titles and scores.
2) User can tap “Roast it again (harder/softer)” which reuses the same submission with a different intensity setting.
3) User can reply with “What if we target X instead?” and the bot offers a delta-roast focused on the changed assumption.

7) Moderation and abuse handling
1) If user attempts to force toxicity or personal attacks, the bot refuses and proceeds with a safer critique.
2) If the user spams, bot rate-limits and explains when they can try again.
3) Admin-only controls allow limiting/banning abusive users and reviewing usage metrics.

Tech stack:
Runtime and bot framework
1) Node.js (ESM) single service process.
2) grammY for Telegram update handling, middleware, and conversational flows.
3) Configuration via environment variables, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.

Persistence
1) MongoDB (already integrated) for durable storage of users, submissions, roasts, and moderation state.
2) In-memory maps for short-lived session state and cooldowns (with TTL cleanup) to keep flows responsive.

External integrations (kept generic)
1) A text generation provider to produce the roast; the bot is designed so the provider can be swapped without changing business logic.
2) Optional: object storage or simple filesystem storage only if you later support user-uploaded screenshots or cached page snapshots; otherwise not required.

HTTP and content retrieval
1) A simple HTTP client for fetching public web pages with strict size/time limits.
2) An HTML-to-text extraction approach to derive headline/CTA/trust signals for the UX critique.

Deployment and operations
1) Run as a Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Use either long polling or webhook mode depending on your environment; the architecture supports both.
3) Centralized logs and basic metrics exported from the process (latency, error rates, rate-limits, generation time).

Non-functional requirements:
Security and privacy
1) Minimize stored data: store only what’s needed for history and abuse prevention; allow users to disable history.
2) Treat links as untrusted input: enforce allow/deny rules (e.g., block local network targets), timeouts, and response size caps.
3) Protect tokens and secrets via environment variables; avoid printing sensitive content in logs.

Safety and tone guarantees
1) The bot never targets immutable characteristics or the user personally; it critiques the product and messaging.
2) Built-in refusal patterns for requests to harass, doxx, or generate hateful content.
3) If user requests “more savage,” the bot increases bluntness and humor, not cruelty.

Performance and scalability
1) Limit concurrent roasts per process to avoid memory spikes; queue internally using in-process concurrency control.
2) Cache link fetch results briefly (content hash + TTL) to avoid repeated downloads during “roast again.”
3) Keep responses compact and consistent; always enforce the 500-word maximum.

Reliability and maintainability
1) Clear error handling paths: link fetch failure, generation timeout, Telegram API errors.
2) Idempotent handling of updates and retries.
3) Structured logging with correlation ids per submission.

Product quality
1) Output consistency enforced by schema-like validation (section presence, word count, actionable items).
2) A/B testable system prompts and tone settings stored in config so you can iterate without redeploying frequently.
3) Basic analytics: counts of roasts, average score distribution, most common red flags (derived tags) to improve prompts and UX.

Bot commands:
• /start — Explain what the bot does, set expectations, and guide the first roast. (usage: /start) [Onboarding]
• /roast — Start a guided flow to submit your project and receive a structured roast. (usage: /roast) [Core]
• /settings — Configure roast intensity, language, link fetching, and history privacy. (usage: /settings) [Configuration]
• /intensity — Quickly change roast intensity (mild, medium, savage). (usage: /intensity mild|medium|savage) [Configuration]
• /template — Get a one-message submission template you can paste and fill in. (usage: /template) [Core]
• /history — View your recent roasts and re-run one with different intensity. (usage: /history) [Utility]
• /redo — Re-roast your last submission (optionally with a new intensity). (usage: /redo [mild|medium|savage]) [Core]
• /deletemydata — Delete stored history and disable future storage (optional privacy mode). (usage: /deletemydata) [Privacy]
• /help — Show usage tips, examples of good submissions, and limits. (usage: /help) [Onboarding]
• /status — Show service status hints (rate limits, last roast time) for the user. (usage: /status) [Utility]

2) Add a cleanup job within the process (timer-based) to purge old submissions/roasts past retention (e.g., 30–90 days) when enabled.
planSections.keyFlows:
1) Onboarding and tone calibration
1) /start explains what the bot does, the “slightly savage but constructive” promise, and the 500-word constraint.
2) User chooses intensity (default medium). The bot clarifies that insults are not allowed; critiques are about the product.
3) Bot suggests the submission format and offers a “one-message template.”

2) Guided submission (recommended)
1) /roast starts a session.
2) Bot asks for: what it is (1–2 sentences), who it’s for, what pain it solves, why it’s different, how it makes money, and optional link.
3) Bot validates minimum completeness (e.g., at least description + target user). If missing, it asks a single clarifying question.
4) Bot confirms: “I’ll roast this with intensity X; link analysis Y; under 500 words.”

3) Single-message submission (fast path)
1) User sends a paragraph and a link.
2) Bot parses the message, extracts link, detects missing core fields.
3) If fields are missing, it asks at most one follow-up question to avoid user fatigue.

4) Link critique (when provided)
1) Bot fetches the page with strict limits: small maximum bytes, short timeout, and a safe user agent.
2) Bot extracts key positioning elements: headline clarity, subheadline support, CTA presence, trust signals, pricing visibility, and friction.
3) Bot integrates findings into the roast under a dedicated UX/positioning section.
4) If fetch fails, bot says so plainly and critiques based on the user-provided description only.

5) Roast generation and enforcement
1) Bot generates a structured roast with required components:
1) Strong first impression
2) Problem clarity
3) Differentiation and switching motivation
4) Business model realism
5) Target market focus
6) UX/positioning critique (if link)
7) Single biggest red flag
8) 3–5 specific actionable improvements
9) Roast Score (1–10)
10) Mic-drop line
2) Post-processing checks:
1) Word count <= 500
2) Contains all required sections
3) No personal attacks; no hate/harassment
4) Action items are concrete (e.g., change headline, add pricing, narrow ICP)
3) If a check fails, the bot performs a constrained rewrite pass and re-validates.

6) History, sharing, and iteration
1) /history lists recent roasts with short titles and scores.
2) User can tap “Roast it again (harder/softer)” which reuses the same submission with a different intensity setting.
3) User can reply with “What if we target X instead?” and the bot offers a delta-roast focused on the changed assumption.

7) Moderation and abuse handling
1) If user attempts to force toxicity or personal attacks, the bot refuses and proceeds with a safer critique.
2) If the user spams, bot rate-limits and explains when they can try again.
3) Admin-only controls allow limiting/banning abusive users and reviewing usage metrics.
planSections.techStack:
Runtime and bot framework
1) Node.js (ESM) single service process.
2) grammY for Telegram update handling, middleware, and conversational flows.
3) Configuration via environment variables, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.

Persistence
1) MongoDB (already integrated) for durable storage of users, submissions, roasts, and moderation state.
2) In-memory maps for short-lived session state and cooldowns (with TTL cleanup) to keep flows responsive.

External integrations (kept generic)
1) A text generation provider to produce the roast; the bot is designed so the provider can be swapped without changing business logic.
2) Optional: object storage or simple filesystem storage only if you later support user-uploaded screenshots or cached page snapshots; otherwise not required.

HTTP and content retrieval
1) A simple HTTP client for fetching public web pages with strict size/time limits.
2) An HTML-to-text extraction approach to derive headline/CTA/trust signals for the UX critique.

Deployment and operations
1) Run as a Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Use either long polling or webhook mode depending on your environment; the architecture supports both.
3) Centralized logs and basic metrics exported from the process (latency, error rates, rate-limits, generation time).
planSections.nonFunctional:
Security and privacy
1) Minimize stored data: store only what’s needed for history and abuse prevention; allow users to disable history.
2) Treat links as untrusted input: enforce allow/deny rules (e.g., block local network targets), timeouts, and response size caps.
3) Protect tokens and secrets via environment variables; avoid printing sensitive content in logs.

Safety and tone guarantees
1) The bot never targets immutable characteristics or the user personally; it critiques the product and messaging.
2) Built-in refusal patterns for requests to harass, doxx, or generate hateful content.
3) If user requests “more savage,” the bot increases bluntness and humor, not cruelty.

Performance and scalability
1) Limit concurrent roasts per process to avoid memory spikes; queue internally using in-process concurrency control.
2) Cache link fetch results briefly (content hash + TTL) to avoid repeated downloads during “roast again.”
3) Keep responses compact and consistent; always enforce the 500-word maximum.

Reliability and maintainability
1) Clear error handling paths: link fetch failure, generation timeout, Telegram API errors.
2) Idempotent handling of updates and retries.
3) Structured logging with correlation ids per submission.

Product quality
1) Output consistency enforced by schema-like validation (section presence, word count, actionable items).
2) A/B testable system prompts and tone settings stored in config so you can iterate without redeploying frequently.
3) Basic analytics: counts of roasts, average score distribution, most common red flags (derived tags) to improve prompts and UX.

envVarDocs.title: Telegram Bot Token
envVarDocs.description: Used to connect to the Telegram Bot API.
envVarDocs.usage: Read at startup; required to boot.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Create a bot with @BotFather and copy the token.

envVarDocs.title: CookMyBots AI Endpoint
envVarDocs.description: Base URL for the CookMyBots AI Gateway. Must be a base URL (no /chat suffix).
envVarDocs.usage: The bot calls POST {COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT}/chat via src/lib/ai.js.

envVarDocs.title: CookMyBots AI Key
envVarDocs.description: Authorization key for the CookMyBots AI Gateway.
envVarDocs.usage: Sent as Authorization: Bearer COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY on gateway requests.

envVarDocs.title: MongoDB Connection URI
envVarDocs.description: Optional. Enables long-term memory in the memory_messages collection. If missing, the bot falls back to in-memory storage without crashing.
envVarDocs.usage: Stores conversation turns and retrieves the most recent 10–20 turns as context.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Create a MongoDB database (e.g., MongoDB Atlas) and copy the connection string.

envVarDocs.title: AI Timeout
envVarDocs.description: Optional. Timeout in milliseconds for CookMyBots AI calls.
envVarDocs.usage: Used by src/lib/ai.js AbortController timeout; defaults to 600000.

envVarDocs.title: Public Base URL
envVarDocs.description: Optional. Not used by core roast flow, but kept for platform compatibility and future link construction needs.
envVarDocs.usage: Currently unused; safe to leave empty.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Often provided by the deploy platform after deploy.

envVarDocs.title: Max Reply Length
envVarDocs.description: Optional. Hard cap for assistant replies to keep them fast to read on Telegram.
envVarDocs.usage: Agent truncates output safely at paragraph/sentence boundaries; defaults to 1200.
